Francesco Brancaccio and Carlo Vercellone

Birth, Death, and Resurrection
of the Issue of the Common:
A Historical and Theoretical Perspective




                                                                                  Downloaded from http://read.dukeupress.edu/south-atlantic-quarterly/article-pdf/118/4/699/692147/1180699.pdf by guest on 15 December 2021
                      T  he common is a long-running story that pre-
                      cedes, traverses, and extends beyond the Industrial
                      Revolution and modernity with a strong and unex-
                      pected resurgence in contemporary capitalism. It is
                      therefore useful to open this issue of SAQ by briefly
                      recalling the key developments of this historical
                      journey and the questions it raised to better under-
                      stand the origin, meaning, and issues at stake in
                      the current debate.
                            In reality, forms of management and owner-
                      ship based on common goods precede those based
                      on the public and the private. Different economic
                      and social systems prior to the advent of capitalism
                      relied on institutions and methods of organizing
                      production based on forms of common property of
                      the land and other natural resources.1 The hunt-
                      er-gatherer communities theorized by the father of
                      modern anthropology, Lewis Henry Morgan
                      (1999), and at the center of a stimulating essay by
                      Marshall Sahlins (1972), show some significant
                      similarities with the land commons that the Nobel
                      Prize winner for economics Elinor Ostrom pro-
                      posed between the end of the twentieth and the
                      beginning of the twenty-first century.
                            The same applies to the so-called primitive
                      communist or communitarian modes of production

                      The South Atlantic Quarterly 118:4, October 2019
                      doi 10.1215/00382876-7825551 © 2019 Duke University Press
700   The South Atlantic Quarterly • October 2019



(Amin 1973) that originated in the Neolithic revolution with the develop-
ment of agriculture and livestock farming. The rules for organizing socie-
ties were based on principles of enforcement of the common, which show
numerous analogies with the principles of management and ownership of
Ostrom’s common-pool resources: collective property guarantees all members
of the community access to the land, but according to precise rules that con-




                                                                                   Downloaded from http://read.dukeupress.edu/south-atlantic-quarterly/article-pdf/118/4/699/692147/1180699.pdf by guest on 15 December 2021
cern both the use and the distribution of the product among members of the
community. In this sense, we could even ask whether the natural commons
described by the new political economics of the Indiana school2 are nothing
more than the remains of a historical period in which they performed a pre-
dominant role.
       Engels (2001: 34), in The Origin of the Family, Private Property and the
State, in the contradictions generated by the development of the social divi-
sion of labor and productivity, identified one of the main factors that led to
the crisis of the communitarian models giving rise to the creation of the
state and ownership “thereby freely developing those class antagonisms and
class struggles that make up the contents of all written history up to the pres-
ent time.” This schema, however, is certainly marred by a certain economic
determinism.
       Proof of this is the way in which even during the economic and social
formations in which the institutions of the public and the private began to be
established, the common, as “property” for collective use of the land, contin-
ued to perform a key and sometimes dominant role alongside the state and
the market.
       This is the case, for example, with the Chinese imperial and taxation
system, which was established starting from the first Han dynasty and
which also saw an important development of the market economy long
before Europe, in the ninth century (Aglietta and Bai 2012). Along with pub-
lic property of the main means of production and infrastructure, access to
land and natural resources remained largely regulated by social norms estab-
lished at the community level. The right of abusus remains excluded from
this type of communal property.3 This fact was considered by many histori-
ans as one of the main hindrances to the development of capitalism in
China, since contrary to what happened with the dissolution of the European
feudal system, it obstructed a process of dispossession and proletarianiza-
tion of the workforce. Very similar methods of access to land and natural
resources can also be found in India up to the British colonization and the
resulting establishment of private ownership and its associated production
and distribution systems (Hobsbawm 1997).
     Brancaccio and Vercellone • Birth, Death, and Resurrection of the Commons 701



       In the history of the feudal system in Europe, the emerging structures
of the public and the private slotted together with forms of ownership and
governance based on the common, whether these were a Germanic legacy,
institutions that the Normans exported to Sicily and England, following the
invasions of the first two centuries of the year one thousand, or the fruit of
other local developments. From this perspective, a good example is the way




                                                                                             Downloaded from http://read.dukeupress.edu/south-atlantic-quarterly/article-pdf/118/4/699/692147/1180699.pdf by guest on 15 December 2021
in which, in the formation of the nation state and English constitutionalism,
the promulgation of the Magna Carta in 1215 not only expressed a compro-
mise with the claims of the feudal dominions but also recognized the collec-
tive rights to access and use of communal goods related to the land, which
were established in the Charter of the Forest (Linebaugh 2008; Mattei 2011;
Bollier 2014) to such an extent that Thompson (1971) argued that although
the forest can seem like an uncultivated expanse, in reality it is the site of “a
complex economy,” which for a long time was the basis for what the author
called “the moral economy.”
       Furthermore, as shown by historians of economics, such as Karl Polanyi
(2010), Fernand Braudel (1992), and Immanuel Wallerstein (2011), the crisis
of the feudal system that followed the Black Death and the peasant revolts of
the fourteenth century was accompanied by a process of emancipation from
feudal servitude. In this context, the common lands assume great importance
in the economic and social organization of agriculture in England. The shar-
ing of communal lands was based on forms of democracy organized in the
villages that regulated access to natural goods (forests, pastures, hunting and
fishing reserves) and crop rotation, reconciling them with the objective of
reproduction and intergenerational sustainability, of the commons. This his-
toric phase, in conjunction with the vitality of the cities and the artisanal sys-
tem of self-government of corporations, is in fact remembered as a period of
popular prosperity and represents a sort of Golden Age of the commons.
       As Marx (1976: 877–78) pointed out in the section of Volume 1 of Cap-
ital, dedicated to primitive accumulation:
      In England, serfdom had disappeared in practice by the last part of the four-
      teenth century. The immense majority of the population consisted then, and
      to a still larger extent in the fifteenth century, of free peasant proprietors, how-
      ever much the feudal trappings might disguise their absolute ownership. In
      the larger seigniorial domains, the old bailiff, himself a serf, was displaced by
      the free farmer. The wage-labourers of agriculture were partly peasants who
      made use of their leisure time by working on the large estates, and partly an
      independent, special class of wage-labourer, relatively and absolutely few in
702   The South Atlantic Quarterly • October 2019



      numbers. The latter were also in practice peasants, farming independently for
      themselves, since, in addition to their wages, they were provided with arable
      land to the extent of four or more acres, together with their cottages. More-
      over, like the other peasants, they enjoyed the right to exploit the common
      land, which gave pasture to their cattle, and furnished them with timber, fire-




                                                                                        Downloaded from http://read.dukeupress.edu/south-atlantic-quarterly/article-pdf/118/4/699/692147/1180699.pdf by guest on 15 December 2021
      wood, turf, etc. . . . Such conditions, together with the urban prosperity so
      characteristic of the fifteenth century, permitted the development of that pop-
      ular wealth Chancellor Fortescue depicted so eloquently in his De laudibus
      legum Angliae, but they ruled out wealth in the form of capital.

This description of the Golden Age of the commons, moreover, must be com-
pleted, as demonstrated by feminist historiography (Federici 2004; Dalla
Costa 1975; Karakass 2016), by the role played by women’s autonomous
knowledge in relation to their own bodies and the organization of society.
This situation was in contrast to the deterioration of their condition and the
separation between the spheres of production and reproduction, which
marked the witch hunt as part of the process of primitive accumulation. It was in
large part in reaction to this process of economic and social emancipation
based on the common that, as highlighted by Polanyi (2010) and Braudel
(1992), the enclosures movement and the expulsion of peasants from commu-
nal lands started. It was a fundamental step of the process that led to the
establishment of the principles of absolute private ownership, making the
land into a commodity4 that can be freely bought and sold on the market. It
should be noted that precisely in this historical process we find the real cul-
prit of Garrett Hardin’s (1968) celebrated “tragedy of the commons”: it does
not originate from an absence of rules for managing the common lands, but
from their destruction following the capitalist dynamic of the enclosures,
which especially in the first phase, relied on their conversion into pastures
for intensive sheep farming (Vercellone et al. 2017).
       Not by chance, the objections to the enclosure of communal lands in
England (the country in which sheep eat men, recalling a phrase used by
Thomas More in Utopia, published in 1516, to describe their devastating social
effects) had a resounding effect in the territories of Germany, where a long
tradition of institutions based on common property preexisted.5 In tandem
with events on the other side of the Channel, privatization of common lands
was one of the factors behind the revolt of the peasants who attributed their
land poverty to the large estate owners and the temporal power of the Church.
Significantly, the popular insurrection, which enflamed Germany between
1524 and 1526, chose the rallying cry Omnia sunt communia. Engels himself,
     Brancaccio and Vercellone • Birth, Death, and Resurrection of the Commons 703



in The Peasant War in Germany (2014), made the figure of Thomas Müntzer
(1489–1525), leader of the rebels and promoter of this motto, into one of the
most important revolutionaries in history. He was the antithesis of the figure
of Luther, who instead fought resolutely against “the bands of peasants,”
embodying the logic of the Protestant Reformation as the spirit of capitalism.
       The defeat of the German peasant uprising, along with the defeat of




                                                                                     Downloaded from http://read.dukeupress.edu/south-atlantic-quarterly/article-pdf/118/4/699/692147/1180699.pdf by guest on 15 December 2021
the agrarian communism of the Diggers and the Levellers at the time of
Cromwell’s English Revolution, in fact constitute two decisive moments for
the joint affirmation of the figure of the modern nation states and the hege-
mony of the capitalist mode of production. State and market, public and pri-
vate, could thus begin to increasingly relegate the common to the margins of
society and establish themselves as the almost exclusive principles of the
economic and social order. This process was decisively accelerated by the
development of the industrial revolution. Of course, at first the workers’
movement seemed to reinvigorate forms of solidarity, self-governance, and
even ownership based on the common. As André Gorz (2003) noted, it was
the heroic era of trade unions, workers cooperatives, and the mutualism
movement. Financial resources and means of production and consumption
were shared “in the name of the right to life” and on the basis of a notion of
needs that still remains largely foreign to the logic of the market and capital-
ist production, using the persistence of the principles of what Thompson
(1971) calls the moral economy of the commoners.
       It was also the era in which the expansion of capitalism in Russia went
hand in hand with the resurgence of rural communes, in the form of the Mir
communities, which remained in place until the beginning of the twentieth
century.
       Not by chance, we owe some of the last nineteenth-century texts in
which the question of the commons appears explicitly to Marx, and they are
inspired by the question of the problem of the transition to socialism in Rus-
sia. More precisely, from 1867 to his death in 1883, as demonstrated by an
analysis of his correspondence during that period, Marx’s thinking was
really focused on two closely entwined and very topical points: First, the
question of rent land as a central institution of the capitalist mode of extract-
ing value, and reproducing the structural conditions of its existence. That is,
the expropriation of the workforce and the subsumption of life and the total-
ity of nature to capital. Second, the symmetrical and rival question of the com-
mons formulated as a form of resistance to the process of proletarianisation,
but also as the vector for a potentially alternative model of self-organization
of work.
704   The South Atlantic Quarterly • October 2019



       In the Mirs, that is, the Russian agricultural communities, a model of
direct democracy, Marx found, near the end of his life, a crucial link in his
theory. In this context, we should recall that the term Mir, which in Russian
means “assembly,” was the decision-making body for governing the Russian
rural communities called obščina, which means precisely “common.” Now,
the question of the Mirs was at the center of a lively debate on the establish-




                                                                                     Downloaded from http://read.dukeupress.edu/south-atlantic-quarterly/article-pdf/118/4/699/692147/1180699.pdf by guest on 15 December 2021
ment of socialism in Russia within which Marx was directly involved
through his renowned correspondence with Vera Zasulič.
       In this context, dispelling some misunderstandings derived from the
analysis formulated in Volume 1 of Capital, in the celebrated chapter on
primitive accumulation, Marx clarified two essential points for formulating
the transition to a time after capitalism: firstly, there was no deterministic
historical necessity that, as had happened in England, made the destruction
of the commons and the advent of capitalism into a necessary precondition
for the transition to communism, breaking away, on this point, from a notion
of history divided into stages and interspersed by the inevitable succession of
different modes of production; furthermore, the Mirs could embody
“through their further development an alternative way to escape capitalism”
reappropriating the same advanced technologies developed by capitalism at
the dawn of the Second Industrial Revolution.
       It is worth remembering how this evolution of Marx’s approach is
located moreover in the continuity of the lessons learned from the experience
of the Paris Commune designed as a form of “generalized communal self-
government,” according to an analysis that is summarized in the radical criti-
cism of the state notion of socialism set out in the Gotha Programme (1875;
see Marx 1938).
       In short, in the last years of his life, Marx drafted the elements of a
plan of society, which we could summarize with the formula the democracy
of the common against the rent and bureaucratic logic of the state, a formula that
a century and a half later retains, in our opinion, great relevance in light of
the symbiosis between the figure of the neoliberal state and the extractivism
of finance as well as the other forms of income that are arranged around it.
       After this last great phrase from Marx, however, we have to note that
the problem of the common practically disappeared from the theoretical and
political debate of the twentieth century.
       This was in a way squashed by the hegemony of the statist diamat of
socialism and the deepening of the dynamic of real subsumption, which to
Rosa Luxembourg made the common lands seem like simple residues of
precapitalist social forms destined ineluctably to be devoured by the accu-
mulation of capital on a global scale.
     Brancaccio and Vercellone • Birth, Death, and Resurrection of the Commons 705



       Therefore, after the last efforts that span the Great Depression of the
1930s, in countries with an advanced stage of capitalism, the development
of the institutions of the Keynesian state tended to absorb into the bureau-
cratic logic of the public the same forms of solidarity that the mutualism
and cooperative movements had begun to create in response to a progres-
sive desocialization of the economy. During the Fordist growth of the post-




                                                                                     Downloaded from http://read.dukeupress.edu/south-atlantic-quarterly/article-pdf/118/4/699/692147/1180699.pdf by guest on 15 December 2021
war boom, the dialectic of the public and the private became so all-encom-
passing that the organization of solidarity seemed to be increasingly taken
over by the bureaucratic organization of the welfare state. The historical
sense of the concept of the common thus seemed to fall definitively into the
oblivion of history.
       Until the second half of the 1960s, in the same economic theory there
are only two types of goods and two methods of management: private goods
(rivalrous and excludable through pricing) managed and produced by corpo-
rations considered as the only organization capable of embodying the princi-
ples of economic efficiency; public goods (non-rivalrous and not-excludable),
which are no more than a failure of the market, that is, an anomaly managed
vertically by the state.
       And when the term commons reappeared, with Hardin’s (1968)
renowned article, it was only to emphasize the impossibility of another form
of resource management compared to its own principles of exclusive owner-
ship for both the public and the private. The story was not, however, a linear
process, but proceeded through hybridizations, breaks, and bifurcations.
       Following the social movements at the start of the crisis of industrial
capitalism and the emergence of a capitalism based on knowledge and digi-
tal technology, the problem of the common resurfaced in academic literature
as the concrete dynamic, lifting little by little the same veil that was placed
over its historical role. The monolithic representation of the functioning of
the economy and society based on the state-market, public-private formulas,
was gradually called into question. This return to strength of the common
was rooted in three main conflictual transformations in which the bot-
tom-up development of the commons was intertwined with the new enclo-
sures of knowledge, of land, and those living under global capitalism.
       The first transformation is linked to the meeting between the forma-
tion of a widespread intelligence and the information revolution that has
driven the movement of the democracy of the commons towards experiment-
ing with knowledge-intensive communities that bring alternative forms of
coordination to both the state and the market, in their principles of manage-
ment and ownership (Wiki models, free software, platform cooperativism,
etc.). The whole story of the digital revolution is, moreover, an illustration of
706 The South Atlantic Quarterly • October 2019



this crucial aspect. From the conception of the personal computer and the
merger of Arpanet and Usenet up to the formulation of the web protocols
transferred by Tim Berners-Lee into the public domain, the open nature of
information technologies and the principles of internet neutrality are the
product of a social construction of the commons in permanent conflict with
both the logic of the state and the ownership and extraction logic of platform




                                                                                   Downloaded from http://read.dukeupress.edu/south-atlantic-quarterly/article-pdf/118/4/699/692147/1180699.pdf by guest on 15 December 2021
capitalism.
       The second transformation is linked to the manner in which the devel-
opment of the feminist movement has unveiled “the arcane of reproduction”
(Fortunati 1996) and the close link between so-called productive and repro-
ductive labor. Contemporary production increasingly takes on the peculiar
features that have historically marked the sphere of reproductive activity.
Contemporary work, as it is relational, affective, linguistic work, in fact
delineates the terms of a feminization of work. It embodies, at the same
time, the dynamics that lead to new forms of exploitation and the establish-
ment of the reproductive common, exploring therein, including in the pub-
lic services, cooperative models, which, as with the model of the Buurtzorg
nurses, directly involve users in a practice of commoning.
       The third transformation (in which the tension between Capitalocene
and biopolitics is presented in dramatic terms) is undoubtedly linked to the
ecological crisis and the recognition of the finitude of the planet. This aware-
ness pushes the theme of the common lands back into prominence and with
it the wealth of so-called ancestral knowledge (Shiva 2002) as instruments of
anti-productivist agriculture capable of preserving scarce and non-renewable
resources. But beware. The common lands are not only a premodern and pre-
capitalist residue. Instead, they constitute the expression of the vitality of a
whole series of holistic knowledges of nature destined to perform an increas-
ingly central role for finding a way to overcome the environmental crisis. A
decisive aspect of common development today is in fact the synergy and the
hybridization between traditional agronomic knowledge and the search for
new techniques that economize energy to guarantee respect for the environ-
ment and the nutritional quality of products. It is a key point as much in
regions that were industrialized a long time ago as in the so-called emerging
economies where, as theorized by Correa’s government in Ecuador, this ques-
tion should have been put at the center of the construction of a new produc-
tive matrix likely to ensure the transition from a first-exporter economy to a
social economy of knowledge liberated from the logic of cognitive capitalism.
       In short, the resurgence of the commons is not, except minimally, a
return to the past. Instead it aims at the construction of a society of general
intellect, which could potentially overcome the order of goods and profit. The
     Brancaccio and Vercellone • Birth, Death, and Resurrection of the Commons 707



central issue is, therefore, to consider the role the common could play in
overcoming the socioeconomic and environmental crisis that permeates
capitalism and which, as Gorz (2003) noted, shakes the epistemic founda-
tions of the culture of progress on which it based its historical legitimacy.
       It is here, through this question, that we can also find a key criterion to
guide us through the plurality of analyses that have been proposed in rela-




                                                                                     Downloaded from http://read.dukeupress.edu/south-atlantic-quarterly/article-pdf/118/4/699/692147/1180699.pdf by guest on 15 December 2021
tion to the meaning and issue of the phenomenon of the rebirth of the com-
mons. From this perspective and in very simple terms, we can identify two
major lines of interpretation. In the first trend, the problem of the commons
is claimed to essentially correspond to the necessary discovery or rediscovery
of the existence and socioeconomic utility of a variety of forms of ownership
and governance that are richer than the traditional one centered on the pub-
lic-private formula. This first position finds its theoretical substrate in the
school of Elinor Ostrom and in her way of combining the instruments of the
neo-institutionalist political economy and those of legal realism associated
with the bundle of rights approach. An analogous positioning can be found
in the digital libertarianism of the economy of networks theorized by authors
such as Yochai Benkler (2007). Both these approaches in fact share the the-
sis according to which the vocation of the common is not to be an element of
radical objection to capitalism capable of questioning its primacy. Instead, it
involves establishing new methods of regulation able to recognize the plural-
ity of the forms of governance and ownership, and facilitate its complemen-
tary role in the context of capitalism.
       In the other series of approaches, the common is claimed to constitute,
instead, a potentially alternative system to capitalism, which would replace it
as the dominant principle. The interlocutors and the sources of most thought
in these approaches are therefore naturally the social movements, which
establish the common as a principle of transformation and self-governance
of society. From the problem of the common in the singular as a political
principle and/or as a mode of production to a return to instruments used in
the theory of the holons systems, this perspective feeds an extremely rich
idea for overcoming the impasses of the historical models of self-manage-
ment and for reformulating the corporation itself as a productive common.
At the same time, this perspective marks an epistemological turning point
in the legal sciences affirming, regardless of public and private law, the
autonomy of a law of the commons that finds its source first in the common-
ers’ practices of self-government and social experimentation.
       It is to this second line of thinking that this issue of SAQ will be mainly
dedicated, bringing together a plurality of contributions that share, even in
the singularity of each approach, this common research perspective.
708     The South Atlantic Quarterly • October 2019



Notes
 1      For a thorough presentation of these forms, see Max Weber (2003).
 2      The reference is to the university where Elinor Ostrom, with her husband and col-
        league, Vincent Ostrom, in 1973 founded the workshop in Political Theory and Policy
        Analysis, giving rise to a series of studies on so-called rivalrous but non-exclusive com-
        munal goods, such as pastures and fishing reserves.




                                                                                                     Downloaded from http://read.dukeupress.edu/south-atlantic-quarterly/article-pdf/118/4/699/692147/1180699.pdf by guest on 15 December 2021
 3      This social breakdown and distribution of different elements of private ownership is
        part of the basis for the forms of ownership associated with the commons. This observa-
        tion is true for both the most traditional and oldest forms of the commons, such as
        those linked to the land of imperial China or the common land in Europe at the end of
        the medieval era, and for the most modern forms linked to the information revolution,
        as is the case with copyleft.
 4      A fictitious good, in the sense meant by Karl Polanyi (2010: 93–94), since the land, like
        other natural resources, is not a man-made product, but a gift from nature.
 5      According to Marx, it is precisely the Germanic model of the commons “in which cul-
        tivated land belongs as private property to the cultivators, while at the same time, the
        forests, pastures, uncultivated lands, etcetera, remain communal property, that, follow-
        ing the fall of the Roman empire, was introduced by the Germans in all conquered
        countries and, thanks to the traits inherited from its prototype, it became throughout
        the middle ages the only driver behind freedom and popular life” (Marx 1881).

        don: Penguin.
Mattei, Ugo. 2011. Beni comuni. Un manifesto (Common Goods: A Manifesto). Roma-Bari: Laterza.

        Savagery, Through Barbarism to Civilization. New York: Adegi Graphics LLC.

        2nd ed. Boston, MA: Beacon.

        teenth Century.” Past and Present 50, no. 1: 76–136.

        comune come modo di produzione. Per una critica dell’economia política (The Commons as
        a Mode of Production: For a Critique of the Political Economy of Common Goods). Verona:
        Ombre Corte.
